using-imports-and-helper-files.label: Using Imports and Helper Files
using-imports-and-helper-files.summary: Use import statements and multi-file helper modules to build small reusable Python programs.

q9.title: Build an explorer badge from helpers

q1.title: Choose the import style
q1.prompt: Which line imports the whole `helpers` module so you can call `helpers.clean_name("ava")`?
q1.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: `import helpers` imports the whole module, so calls use the module name and a dot, like `helpers.clean_name(...)`.
help.hint_1: Look for the answer that imports the module name itself.
help.hint_2: The direct-import form starts with `from`, but the whole-module form starts with `import`.

q2.title: Return vs print in a helper
q2.prompt: Why is a helper function usually more reusable when it returns a value instead of only printing it?
q2.hint: Think about whether the result can be stored or combined later.

help.concept: A returned value can be used by other code, assigned to a variable, passed into another function, or printed later. Printed output is shown immediately but not passed back as a usable result.
help.hint_1: If another part of the program needs the cleaned text, it must receive that text back from the function.
help.hint_2: Choose the option about using the result in later expressions, not just showing it in the output panel.

options.a: Because returned values can be stored and used by other code
options.b: Because `return` always makes code run faster
options.c: Because printed text can never appear in the output panel
options.d: Because helper functions are only allowed to use `return`

q3.title: Good reasons to use helper functions
q3.prompt: Choose all statements that are good reasons to move repeated logic into a helper function.
q3.hint: Look for benefits related to reuse and readability.

help.concept: Helper functions reduce repetition, make main code easier to read, and give one place to update shared logic.
help.hint_1: If the same cleanup steps appear in several places, a helper can hold them once.
help.hint_2: Pick the options about avoiding repeated code and making the main program clearer.

options.a: You can reuse the same logic in more than one place
options.b: The main program becomes easier to read
options.c: You must always use a helper for every single line of code
options.d: Helper functions remove the need to call functions

q4.title: Valid ways to call imported code
q4.prompt: Suppose a helper module has a function named `double`. Which calls are valid after the matching import? Choose all correct answers.
q4.hint: Match each call style to the import style that makes it possible.

help.concept: After `import helpers`, you call `helpers.double(4)`. After `from helpers import double`, you call `double(4)`. The call must match the imported name.
help.hint_1: One valid call uses the module name and a dot. Another valid call uses the function name directly.
help.hint_2: Do not choose forms that reverse the names or put the function before the module with a dot.

q5.title: Order the steps of a helper call
q5.prompt: Put these steps in the order they happen when code runs: `name = helpers.clean_name("  ava  ")`.
q5.hint: Start with the import already done, then follow the function call and assignment.

help.concept: A function call sends an argument into the helper, the helper computes and returns a value, and then that value is assigned to the variable.
help.hint_1: The text goes into the helper before the variable gets its final value.
help.hint_2: The returned cleaned result comes back before `name` can store it.

tokens.t1: The string `"  ava  "` is passed into `clean_name`
tokens.t2: The helper function processes the text
tokens.t3: The helper returns the cleaned result
tokens.t4: The variable `name` stores that returned result

q6.title: Build a direct import example
q6.prompt: Arrange these lines to make a small program that imports `greet`, calls it, and prints the result.
q6.hint: Arrange the code pieces in the order Python should run them.

help.concept: A direct import usually comes first, then the function call stores a result, then the program prints that result.
help.hint_1: Start with the import line so Python knows where `greet` comes from.
help.hint_2: Call the helper before printing the stored message.

q7.title: Complete a module import call
q7.prompt: Fill in the missing code to call the function through the module name.
q7.hint: Use the module name before the dot.

help.concept: When you import a whole module, you access its functions with `module_name.function_name(...)`.
help.hint_1: The missing part is not the function name; it is the module name used before `.clean_name`.
help.hint_2: Choose the name that would come from `import the missing term`.

q8.title: Complete a direct import line
q8.prompt: Fill in the missing word to import one function directly from `helpers`.
q8.hint: This import style starts with a keyword that names the source module after it.

help.concept: The syntax `from helpers import greet` imports one function directly from a module.
help.hint_1: The missing word comes before the module name.
help.hint_2: Choose the Python keyword used for direct imports from a module.

q9.title: Build an explorer badge from helpers
q9.prompt: The workspace has `helpers/badges.py` with `clean_name` and `make_badge`. In `main.py`, read one name with `input()`, clean it with the helper, build the badge with the helper, and print the badge.
q9.hint: Import the helper functions, then let `main.py` focus on reading input and printing the final badge.

help.concept: A helper module keeps reusable functions in another file. `main.py` can import those helpers and combine them into a small program.
help.hint_2: Store the cleaned name before passing it to `make_badge`.

q9.starterCode:
from helpers.badges import clean_name, make_badge

raw_name = input()
# Clean the name, build a badge, and print it.

q10.title: Use a score helper module
q10.prompt: The workspace has `helpers/score_tools.py`. In `main.py`, import the module as `score_tools`, read a name and score, then print the line returned by `score_tools.score_line(name, score)`.
q10.hint: This task uses the whole-module import style, so calls should start with `score_tools.`.

help.concept: `import helpers.score_tools as score_tools` gives the module a short name. You then call functions through that module name.
help.hint_2: Call `score_tools.score_line(name, score)` and print the returned string.

q10.starterCode:
import helpers.score_tools as score_tools

name = input().strip().title()
score = int(input())
# Use score_tools.score_line(name, score), then print the result.

q11.title: Combine two helper modules
q11.prompt: Use helpers from two files. Import `clean_name` from `helpers.badges` and `rank` from `helpers.score_tools`. Read a name and score, then print `Mission report: NAME unlocked RANK status`.
q11.hint: Use one helper to clean the name and another helper to classify the score.

help.concept: Larger programs often combine helpers from more than one module. Each helper does one small job, and `main.py` connects the pieces.

q11.starterCode:
from helpers.badges import clean_name
from helpers.score_tools import rank

raw_name = input()
score = int(input())
# Build and print: Mission report: NAME unlocked RANK status

sk1.title: Why use helper modules?
sk1.bodyMarkdown:
When a program starts to repeat the same logic, it helps to move that logic into a small helper module. Then your main program can stay focused on the big picture, while helper functions handle smaller jobs.

A helper module is just a place for reusable functions. You can import the whole module or import specific functions from it.

Here is a simple example:

```python
import helpers

name = helpers.clean_name("  aVA  ")
print(name)
```

If `clean_name` trims spaces and fixes capitalization, the output would be:

```python
Ava
```

Step by step:

- `import helpers` makes the helper module available.
- `helpers.clean_name(...)` calls a function stored in that module.
- `print(name)` shows the returned value in the output panel.

This is useful because you can reuse `clean_name` in many places instead of rewriting the same cleanup code.

Try it yourself: imagine you also need to clean city names. Would you rather copy the cleanup code twice, or call the same helper function twice?

sk2.title: Two common import styles
sk2.bodyMarkdown:
Python has two beginner-friendly import styles you will see often.

### Import the module

```python
import helpers

print(helpers.double(4))
```

This style keeps the module name in front, so it is clear where the function came from.

### Import a specific function

```python
from helpers import double

print(double(4))
```

This style lets you call the function directly without writing `helpers.` each time.

Worked example:

```python
from helpers import greet

message = greet("Mina")
print(message)
```

Line by line:

- `from helpers import greet` brings in only the `greet` function.
- `message = greet("Mina")` calls the function and stores its return value.
- `print(message)` displays that value.

Both styles can work well. Early on, `import helpers` can make code easier to read because you can clearly see which names come from the helper module.

Your turn: rewrite `print(helpers.double(4))` using the `from helpers import double` style.

sk3.title: Return values make helpers reusable
sk3.bodyMarkdown:
Helper functions are most useful when they **return** values instead of only printing them. A returned value can be stored, combined, or printed later.

Example:

```python
def clean_name(text):
    return text.strip().title()

name = clean_name("  sam  ")
print("Hello, " + name)
```

The output is:

```python
Hello, Sam
```

Step by step:

- `clean_name` receives `text`.
- `strip()` removes extra spaces at the start and end.
- `title()` changes the capitalization.
- `return` sends the cleaned text back.
- The main code uses that returned value in a greeting.

If the function only printed the cleaned name, it would be harder to reuse inside another expression.

Try this: change the input to `"  rILEY  "` and predict what the output panel will show.
